title_en: Methodology and Structure of Muruj al-Dhahab
content_en:
Muruj al-Dhahab wa Ma'adin al-Jawhar — Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems — is al-Mas'udi's most famous work, and it represents a distinctive approach to historical writing that resists easy classification. It is not primarily a political chronicle or a dynastic history, though it contains extensive narrative about caliphs and dynasties. It is not primarily a geographical work, though it contains rich geographical information about the known world. It is not primarily a natural history, though it engages seriously with questions about the natural world. It is all of these things simultaneously, organized by al-Mas'udi's characteristic method of associating topics through curiosity rather than strict genre conventions.

The work begins with cosmological and geographical matters — the structure of the earth, the seas and rivers, the nature of the known continents, the peoples of the world and their characteristics. This framing reflects al-Mas'udi's conviction that history must be understood in its environmental and cosmological context. Human societies are shaped by their geography, their climate, and their place in the created order, and a history that ignores these dimensions is incomplete.

The historical narrative then moves through the pre-Islamic world — the ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, Persia, India, and the Mediterranean — before reaching the history of the Arabs and the rise of Islam. The Islamic period is treated through a series of accounts of the caliphs, from Abu Bakr through the early Abbasid period, with digressions into the societies, cultures, and natural wonders encountered by al-Mas'udi in his travels.

Al-Mas'udi's style is lively and engaging, combining narrative, anecdote, observation, and reflection in a way that sets him apart from the more austere chronicles of his contemporaries. He quotes poetry, records conversations, describes exotic fauna, and speculates freely about the causes of historical change. This expansive method makes Muruj al-Dhahab one of the most readable works of classical Islamic historical writing.
content_id:
Muruj al-Dhahab wa Ma'adin al-Jawhar — Padang Emas dan Tambang Permata — adalah karya al-Mas'udi yang paling terkenal dan yang paling dapat diakses oleh pembaca modern. Judul tersebut bersifat metaforis: ini adalah koleksi informasi berharga tentang sejarah dan geografi dunia, dipersembahkan seperti tambang yang mengandung permata dan logam mulia. Karya tersebut disusun antara 332 H (943 M) dan 345 H (956 M), dengan al-Mas'udi merevisi dan memperbarui teks tersebut hingga mendekati kematiannya.

Struktur Muruj al-Dhahab mencerminkan ambisi ensiklopedis al-Mas'udi. Teks dimulai dengan kosmologi dan geografi — penciptaan dunia, iklim, lautan, sungai — sebelum bergerak ke sejarah nabi-nabi pra-Islam dan raja-raja kuno. Kemudian mencakup sejarah Islam dari awalnya melalui periode Abbasiyah, diselingi dengan penggalan-penggalan geografis, etnografis, dan filosofis. Struktur ini tidak linier tetapi asosiatif: al-Mas'udi membangun koneksi antara topik-topik, sering menyimpang dari narasi utama untuk mendiskusikan sesuatu yang dipicu oleh peristiwa atau tempat yang baru saja disebutkan.

Metode al-Mas'udi adalah observasi dan wawancara di samping pembacaan tekstual. Ketika beliau mendiskusikan India, beliau melakukannya berdasarkan kunjungan langsung; ketika beliau mendiskusikan Afrika Timur, beliau dapat bercerita dari pengalaman pelayarannya di sana. Informan yang beliau wawancarai termasuk pedagang, pelaut, ulama, pejabat, dan orang-orang biasa — seseorang yang diperlakukan pengetahuannya tentang suatu topik sebagai sumber yang sah terlepas dari statusnya.

Kualitas-kualitas ini memberikan Muruj al-Dhahab sebuah karakter yang berbeda dari banyak teks sejarah Arab lainnya dari periodenya. Sementara banyak sejarawan Muslim abad pertengahan sangat bergantung pada penulis terdahulu untuk informasi tentang masa lalu, al-Mas'udi secara aktif menambahkan informasi baru berdasarkan observasi dan penyelidikan personalnya. Pendekatannya jauh lebih dekat ke apa yang sekarang dikenal sebagai sejarah berbasis bukti daripada sejarah berbasis transmisi yang lazim di antara ulama-ulama Islam lainnya.

Bahasa dan gaya Muruj al-Dhahab adalah salah satu dari karya-karya sastra prosa Arab terbesar. Al-Mas'udi menulis dengan kejelasan dan keanggunan yang membuat karya tersebut menyenangkan untuk dibaca terlepas dari isi substantifnya. Ini bukan kebetulan: al-Mas'udi jelas peduli dengan keindahan sastra dari tulisannya sekaligus dengan isi informatifnya. Kombinasi kecanggihan intelektual dan keanggunan sastra ini menjadikan Muruj al-Dhahab sebuah karya yang luar biasa bahkan berdasarkan standar sastra Arab yang tinggi.
content_ar: Muruj al-Dhahab wa Ma'adin al-Jawhar — Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems — is al-Mas'udi's most famous work, and it represents a distinctive approach to historical writing that resists easy classification. It is not primarily a political chronicle or a dynastic history, though it contains extensive narrative about caliphs and dynasties. It is not primarily a geographical work, though it contains rich geographical information about the known الدنيا. It is not primarily a natural history, though it engages seriously with questions about the natural الدنيا. It is all of these things simultaneously, organized by al-Mas'udi's characteristic method of associating topics through curiosity rather than strict genre conventions. The work begins with cosmological and geographical matters — the structure of the earth, the seas and rivers, the nature of the known continents, the peoples of the الدنيا and their characteristics. This framing reflects al-Mas'udi's conviction that history must be understood in its environmental and cosmological context. Human societies are shaped by their geography, their climate, and their place in the created order, and a history that ignores these dimensions is incomplete. The historical narrative then moves through the pre-Islamic الدنيا — the ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, Persia, India, and the Mediterranean — قبل reaching the history of the Arabs and the rise of الإسلام. The Islamic period is treated through a series of accounts of the caliphs, from أبو بكر through the early Abbasid period, with digressions into the societies, cultures, and natural wonders encountered by al-Mas'udi in his travels. Al-Mas'udi's style is lively and engaging, combining narrative, anecdote, observation, and reflection in a الطريق that sets him apart from the more austere chronicles of his contemporaries. He quotes poetry, records conversations, describes exotic fauna, and speculates freely about the causes of historical change. This expansive method makes Muruj al-Dhahab one of the most readable works of الكلاسيكي Islamic historical writing.
